messages.content: What was the first website to go online on the World Wide Web?
The first website to go online on the World Wide Web was info.cern.ch. It was created by Tim Berners-Lee, the inventor of the World Wide Web, and went live on August 6, 1991.
is the site still up today

Yes, the website info.cern.ch is still active today. However, it has been preserved as a symbol and historical artifact of the early days of the World Wide Web rather than being regularly updated with new content. You can visit the website to see how it originally looked and learn more about its significance in the history of the web.
when was the world wide web developed

The World Wide Web was developed in the late 1980s and early 1990s. The initial proposal for the World Wide Web was presented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 while he was working at CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research). He developed the necessary protocols, such as HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) and HTML (Hypertext Markup Language), and created the first web browser and server software. The World Wide Web became publicly available in August 1991 when the first website went online.
how much did internet plans cost back then when the internet was being adopted by the public

In the early days of the internet, when it was being adopted by the public in the 1990s, internet plans varied in cost depending on the region and service provider. However, it's important to note that the cost of internet access was significantly higher than it is today. The prices for internet plans could range from $20 to $50 per month for dial-up internet connections, which were the primary type of internet access during that time. These plans typically offered limited speeds and required the use of telephone lines. High-speed broadband connections, such as DSL or cable, which became available later, were more expensive and could cost upwards of $100 per month. However, it is worth noting that these prices are a general estimate, and the actual cost varied depending on the location and specific service provider.
what was the first ISP to offer high-speed broadband connections?

The first Internet Service Provider (ISP) to offer high-speed broadband connections to the public was AT&T WorldNet Service (now AT&T Internet Service). In 1996, AT&T introduced its "Project Pronto" initiative, which aimed to roll out ADSL (Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line) technology to deliver broadband internet access. This marked one of the first mass-market deployments of high-speed internet access, allowing users to enjoy faster speeds and an "always-on" connection. Other ISPs followed suit, and the availability of broadband internet gradually expanded in the late 1990s and early 2000s.
how fast was broadband compared to dial up?

Broadband internet provides significantly faster speeds compared to dial-up connections. Dial-up connections, which were commonly used in the early days of the internet, operated at speeds of up to 56 kilobits per second (Kbps). On the other hand, broadband connections allow for much faster speeds, typically ranging from a few megabits per second (Mbps) to several hundred Mbps or even gigabit speeds (1 Gbps = 1000 Mbps).

To put it in perspective, broadband speeds are typically tens to hundreds of times faster than dial-up. This means that tasks such as web browsing, file downloads, streaming videos, and online gaming are much quicker and more efficient with broadband connections. The introduction of broadband revolutionized the online experience by providing faster and more reliable internet access.
how fast was the first publicly available broadband internet connection, compared to the fastest dial up speed of that time?

The first publicly available broadband internet connections offered speeds that were significantly faster than dial-up connections of that time. Dial-up connections typically operated at speeds of up to 56 kilobits per second (Kbps). The earliest broadband connections, such as DSL (Digital Subscriber Line), offered speeds ranging from 128 kilobits per second (Kbps) up to 1.5 megabits per second (Mbps).

To put it in perspective, even the slowest broadband connections of that time were already more than double the maximum speed of dial-up connections. As broadband technology continued to advance, faster speeds became available, surpassing dial-up by a wider margin. Broadband connections provided faster and more reliable internet access, allowing users to browse the web, download files, and engage in online activities with increased efficiency.
did DSL routers still use a phone line though

Yes, DSL (Digital Subscriber Line) routers still use a phone line for internet connectivity. DSL technology utilizes existing copper telephone lines to transmit digital signals and provide internet access. The phone line is used to connect the DSL router to the DSLAM (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer), which operates at the telephone exchange and acts as the intermediary between the user's modem and the internet service provider's network.

While DSL and phone lines are connected, their signals are sent at different frequencies to allow simultaneous voice and data transmission. This means that the connection can be used for both internet access and telephone calls simultaneously without interference. This made DSL a convenient option for users who wanted to have both a phone line and broadband internet access without requiring separate connections.
what happens if you make a phone call during an online internet session with dial up?

If you make a phone call during an online internet session with dial-up, the internet connection will be disrupted. Dial-up connections utilize the same telephone line for both internet access and voice communication. When you pick up the phone to make a call, it seizes the line, interrupting the data transmission of the dial-up connection. This interruption will result in your internet connection being disconnected, and you will need to reconnect once the phone call has ended.
What if you picked up the phone with an internet session in place with a dial up connection? Will you hear the sounds the modem is making for transferring and downloading data?

If you pick up the phone while an internet session is in progress with a dial-up connection, you will likely hear the sounds made by the modem. Dial-up modems use audible tones and sounds to establish a connection and transmit data over the phone line. These sounds are commonly referred to as handshake tones. 

When you pick up the phone during an active dial-up session, you may hear the modem making various beeps, screeches, and chirping sounds. These sounds indicate the data transfer in progress and the communication between your modem and the remote server. However, it's important to note that picking up the phone can disrupt the connection, resulting in a disconnection from the internet. Therefore, it is generally not recommended to pick up the phone while you are using dial-up internet.
When did cable internet become first available?

Cable internet became first available in the late 1990s. The exact timeline of cable internet availability may vary depending on the region and specific cable Internet Service Providers (ISPs). However, commercial cable internet services began rolling out in the late 1990s as cable television providers started to leverage their existing coaxial cable infrastructure to provide high-speed internet access.

Cable internet offered significantly faster speeds compared to dial-up connections and quickly gained popularity among internet users. The availability of cable internet expanded throughout the early 2000s, and it became one of the primary alternatives to dial-up for residential broadband internet access. Today, cable internet remains a widely used and popular option for high-speed internet connectivity.
Cable vs. fiber, which is better?

Cable and fiber are two different types of internet technologies, and the "better" option depends on various factors and personal preferences.

Cable internet:
- Pros: Cable internet can provide fast speeds, typically ranging from 25 Mbps to 1 Gbps, depending on the plan and provider. It is widely available, as it utilizes existing cable TV infrastructure. Cable internet can handle multiple devices simultaneously without significant performance degradation.
- Cons: Cable internet speeds can fluctuate, particularly during peak usage times, as bandwidth is shared among users in the same neighborhood. Upload speeds may be slower compared to download speeds. The performance can be affected by the distance from your home to the local cable node.

Fiber internet:
- Pros: Fiber internet offers incredibly fast and symmetrical speeds, starting from 100 Mbps and reaching up to 10 Gbps. It provides consistent and reliable performance even during peak hours. The distance from the fiber connection point does not typically affect speeds. Fiber is future-proof, as it can handle increasing bandwidth demands.
- Cons: Fiber infrastructure is not as widely available as cable. Installation and deployment can be more complex and expensive, leading to limited availability in certain areas. Fiber plans can be more costly than cable options.

In summary, if you require consistently high speeds, symmetrical upload and download speeds, and future-proof internet technology, fiber may be the better choice if it is available in your area. However, cable internet can still provide fast speeds and is more widely accessible. It's recommended to check with local providers to determine the availability, speeds, and pricing in your specific location.
